notes.anchor: sent on the twelfth day before the Kalends
notes.note: The Romans dated by counting backward (inclusively) from three fixed points - the Kalends (1st), Nones, and Ides; "the twelfth day before the Kalends" of February is 21 January. Inclusive counting means the named day and the Kalends are both counted.
L. Cornelius Lentulus Crus, one of the two consuls of 49 BC and a leading hardliner against Caesar; Cicero met him at Formiae as the Pompeian command fell into confusion.

notes.anchor: at Luceria and Teanum, and the rest of them in Apulia
notes.note: Pompey was concentrating his cohorts in the towns of Apulia and northern Campania - Larinum, Luceria, Teanum - as he debated whether to make a stand in Italy or cross the Adriatic to Greece.
The aerarium, the state treasury kept in the Temple of Saturn; the consuls fled Rome leaving it (or its reserve fund) locked, but Cicero predicts this will not stop Caesar from helping himself.

notes.anchor: broken by pity for the boys
notes.note: "The boys" are Cicero's son Marcus and his nephew Quintus, whose safety weighs on him as he debates whether to follow Pompey into the war.

Cicero still held proconsular imperium from his Cilician command and so was attended by lictors bearing the fasces; pending the triumph he hoped for, this kept him technically outside the city and constrained his movements.
